planting.note: Plant dormant canes or container plants in spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Encore is a late-season floricane (summer-bearing) raspberry that ripens its primary crop in late summer — later than most summer varieties. In zone 9 summer raspberries struggle with heat; Encore is best suited to zone 9a where cooler conditions at elevation or in coastal areas allow successful production. Provide mulch and afternoon shade in zone 9 to reduce heat stress.

directions: Plant in full sun to light afternoon shade in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Encore is a Cornell University late-season summer raspberry producing very large, conical, firm, dark red berries in the last 2–3 weeks of the summer raspberry season — ripening after most other summer varieties have finished, significantly extending the fresh raspberry harvest into mid-to-late summer. The very large berry size (larger than most summer varieties) and excellent sweet-tart flavor with complex aromatic notes make Encore the highest-quality of the late-season summer raspberry types. In zone 9 summer raspberries face significant challenges from heat — raspberries are most productive in zones 6–7 with cool summers — but in the cooler parts of zone 9a, along the coast, or at modest elevation, Encore can produce a satisfying late-season crop. The late-season ripening of Encore avoids the hottest weeks of summer, giving it better zone 9 performance than earlier varieties. Provide consistent irrigation and mulching to maintain the cool root zone conditions raspberries require.
sources: Cornell University Small Fruit Program — Encore Raspberry Release
Purdue University Extension — Raspberry Varieties for Warm Climates
University of California Cooperative Extension — Raspberry Production in Warm Regions

planting.note: Plant dormant canes or container plants in spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Polana is a primocane (fall-bearing) raspberry that in zone 9 produces a fall crop on first-year canes in September–November — potentially the only practical raspberry crop in the warmest zone 9 areas, as the fall primocane season occurs during cooler fall temperatures. Mow or cut all canes to the ground in late winter for a single annual fall crop.

directions: Plant in full sun to light afternoon shade in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Polana is a Polish-bred, fall-bearing (primocane) raspberry producing its main crop on first-year canes in autumn — earlier than most other fall-bearing varieties, beginning to ripen in August–September in most climates. The earlier fall ripening is a significant advantage because it means more of the crop matures before winter freezes end the season in northern areas, and in zone 9 the earlier ripening of Polana compared to later fall-bearing types like Heritage means the crop starts before the hottest September weather. The firm, conical, dark red berries have excellent flavor — well-balanced sweet-tart with rich raspberry aroma. Polana is one of the most productive fall-bearing varieties per area; the compact, self-supporting primocane habit (no trellis needed) makes it practical for small gardens. In zone 9 fall raspberries are more reliably successful than summer varieties because the October ripening occurs in cooler conditions.
sources: Cornell University Small Fruit Program — Fall-Bearing Raspberry Varieties
University of Minnesota Extension — Polana Raspberry
Oregon State University Extension — Fall-Bearing Raspberry Production

planting.note: Plant dormant canes or rooted tip layers in spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Jewel black raspberry is more heat-tolerant than red raspberry but still struggles in the hottest zone 9 summers. It is best suited to zone 9a or the cooler parts of zone 9b. The floricane crop ripens May–June in zone 9, ahead of peak summer heat, making June the primary harvest window.

directions: Plant in full sun to light afternoon shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Jewel is a Cornell University-released black raspberry (Rubus occidentalis) — an entirely distinct species from red raspberries with a different flavor, deeper color, and more intense aromatic character. Black raspberries produce jet-black, hollow drupes with an intensely rich, complex, wine-like flavor that differs dramatically from the lighter, brighter flavor of red raspberries — the dark color indicates very high anthocyanin content, among the highest of any commonly grown berry. Black raspberry flavor is described as a deep, earthy, complex fruitiness with more tannin and less overt sweetness than red raspberries, making them outstanding for jams, pies, syrup, black raspberry ice cream (a regional specialty in the Ohio River Valley), and liqueurs. Jewel is the leading commercial black raspberry variety, selected for large, firm fruits and good resistance to anthracnose disease. In zone 9 the June ripening precedes peak summer heat, making black raspberry production more feasible than red raspberry in warm climates.
sources: Cornell University Small Fruit Program — Jewel Black Raspberry Release
Ohio State University Extension — Black Raspberry Production
University of Illinois Extension — Black Raspberry Varieties

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. John produces heavier crops when cross-pollinated with Adams or another elderberry variety. In zone 9 it flowers in May–June and produces ripe berries in July–August. John is a vigorous spreader by root suckers in moist soils; mow around the planting to control spread.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile, well-drained to periodically wet soil; John is a named American elderberry cultivar selected at the New York State Agricultural Experiment Station at Geneva as part of the New York State elderberry improvement program — the same program that produced the widely-grown Adams cultivar. John was selected as a pollinator companion for Adams, producing profuse flowers slightly earlier than Adams to ensure cross-pollination during the Adams flowering period. The vigorous, upright shrub produces large, flattened flower corymbs (elderflower clusters) with abundant small white flowers in early summer, followed by heavy clusters of small, dark purple-black berries in late summer. John is particularly valued in mixed elderberry plantings where it improves Adams yields through cross-pollination; the two varieties are the most common pairing in eastern American elderberry plantings. In zone 9 both elderberry flowers and berries are produced abundantly in favorable moist conditions.
sources: New York State Agricultural Experiment Station Geneva — John Elderberry Selection
Penn State Extension — American Elderberry Cultivars
University of Missouri Center for Agroforestry — Elderberry Production Guide

planting.note: Plant dormant cuttings or container-grown vines in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Dixie is a perfect-flowered (self-fertile) muscadine that sets crops without a pollinator vine. In zone 9 it ripens September–October. The large, dark fruit and self-fertile habit make it an excellent standalone planting for gardeners with space for a single muscadine vine.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Dixie is a large, dark-skinned muscadine released by the University of Georgia, producing very large, round, dark purple to black berries with a sweet, rich flavor and thin, relatively tender skin that makes fresh eating more comfortable than older tough-skinned muscadine varieties. As a perfect-flowered (self-fertile) cultivar, Dixie sets heavy crops reliably without a pollinator vine — a significant practical advantage for home gardeners. The late-season ripening (September–October in zone 9) extends the fresh muscadine harvest into fall when most other fresh fruit has finished. The vigorous vines are productive once established and show good resistance to the common muscadine diseases including black rot and angular leaf spot that can be problems in the humid Gulf South. Dixie pairs well with Triumph (bronze) for a complementary planting that provides both dark and bronze-skinned muscadines from the same trellis system.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Dixie Muscadine Cultivar
Mississippi State University Extension — Muscadine Grape Varieties
North Carolina State University Extension — Dark Muscadine Varieties
